frameworks.name: oculomotor inhibition (OMI)
frameworks.one_line: transient suppression of microsaccades and blinks following stimulus onset

frameworks.name: fringe-of-awareness / subliminal perception
frameworks.one_line: backward-masked stimuli barely visible to limit top-down strategies

frameworks.name: implicit / hidden memory recognition
frameworks.one_line: involuntary oculomotor signature extended to familiarity detection

core_question: Is oculomotor inhibition sensitive to stimulus familiarity, even when the stimulus is on the fringe of awareness?
key_assumptions: microsaccades and blinks reflect involuntary attentional/oculomotor state
backward masking suppresses top-down strategies and biases
passive viewing isolates implicit recognition processes
manipulations: familiarity of facial image (1 familiar vs 7 unfamiliar)
visibility level (highly visible vs backward-masked at fringe of awareness)
stimulus type within OMI (microsaccades vs eye-blinks)
key_findings: OMI was occasionally insensitive to familiarity when faces were highly visible
under masking, microsaccade and blink inhibition were prolonged for familiar faces
microsaccade inhibition onset occurred earlier for familiar faces
interpretations: OMI is sensitive to familiarity and can index implicit recognition
involuntary eye movements offer a novel physiological probe of hidden memories
limitations_noted: familiarity effect was unreliable when stimuli were fully visible
